title: 1 Timothy 1
subtitle: The Charge Against False Teaching
prayer_prompt: As Paul warns Timothy against those who teach false doctrines, consider asking God whether you've wandered into 'myths and endless genealogies' — spiritual rabbit holes that distract from love and good conscience.

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — "Charge Certain Persons Not to Teach Different Doctrine"

heb.paragraph: Paul opens with ἐπιταγή (epitagē), a strong term denoting authoritative command rather than informal request. In the Pastorals this word consistently marks Paul’s apostleship as divinely mandated (cf. Tit 1:3). BDAG defines it as a directive issued with authority, distinguishing it from the softer παρακαλέω (appeal). By framing his apostleship as the product of God’s command, Paul establishes that Timothy’s authority in Ephesus is derivative of divine appointment, not human ambition.

A rare compound verb (ἕτερος + διδάσκω) found only in the Pastorals (here and 6:3), meaning to teach something qualitatively other than the apostolic deposit. The prefix ἕτερος implies not merely additional teaching but doctrinally alien content. This is the Pastorals’ signature term for heresy and sets the polemical agenda for the entire letter.

refs.note: Paul’s insistence on a single authoritative gospel and his anathema against those who preach a different one provides the theological precedent for his command against ἑτεροδιδασκαλία here.
The parallel warning against Jewish myths in the letter to Titus confirms that the same type of false teaching was spreading across multiple Pauline churches during this period.
Paul’s farewell speech to the Ephesian elders predicted that savage wolves would arise from within the church—a prophecy now being fulfilled in the very community Timothy oversees.

notes.note: Paul’s apostleship rests on the direct command of God our Savior and Christ Jesus our hope. This is no self-appointed ministry; it is a divine commission that cannot be revoked by human opposition or ecclesiastical politics.
Timothy is Paul’s true son in the faith—not by physical descent but by spiritual paternity. This familial language establishes Timothy’s legitimacy as Paul’s authorized representative in Ephesus.

The false teachers in Ephesus were consumed with speculative theology—myths, genealogies, and endless controversies that produce nothing but arguments. Sound doctrine always leads to godly living; heresy always leads to division.

The goal of Paul’s instruction is love from a pure heart, a good conscience, and sincere faith. Some have wandered from these and turned to meaningless talk. They want to be teachers of the law, but they do not know what they are talking about.

Paul calls himself an apostle by the command of God, not by his own choice. Ministers must always remember that they have been placed in their office by God and therefore must not water down the message entrusted to them. Timothy, as a true child in the faith, inherits the obligations of the apostolic charge.

The false teachers occupy themselves with myths and genealogies—the very sort of vain speculation that puffs up the mind while leaving the heart untouched. True doctrine always issues in love, and any teaching that does not produce holiness and charity is ipso facto foreign to the gospel.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'our hope' (τῆς ἐλπίδος ἡμῶν) applied to Christ Jesus is unusual in Pauline salutations and anticipates the letter’s emphasis on the eschatological hope that sustains faithful ministry (cf. 4:10; 6:14–15).
The term 'myths' (μύθοις) here likely refers to speculative Jewish traditions rather than Greco-Roman mythology, given the association with genealogies and the desire to be law-teachers (v. 7).

mounce.source: William D. Mounce, Pastoral Epistles, WBC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The salutation follows standard Pauline convention but with distinctive vocabulary: ἐπιταγή (command) replaces the more typical θέλημα (will) to underscore the non-negotiable nature of apostolic authority. The designation of God as 'Savior' (σωτήρ) is a hallmark of the Pastorals, occurring ten times across the three letters. This is not evidence against Pauline authorship but reflects the polemical context: the imperial cult applied σωτήρ to Caesar, and Paul co-opts the title for the true God.

The verb παραγγείλῃς (command, charge) indicates that Timothy’s role is authoritative, not advisory. The false teaching involves μῦθοι (myths) and γενεαλογίαι (genealogies), probably referring to speculative Jewish midrashic expansions of OT narratives. The goal (τέλος) of Paul’s charge is love, good conscience, and sincere faith—a triad that defines authentic Christian existence.

The false teachers have 'swerved' (ἀστοχήσαντες, lit. ‘missed the mark’) from these virtues into ματαιολογία (empty talk). They desire to be νομοδιδάσκαλοι (law-teachers) but lack comprehension of what they affirm. This is not a rejection of Torah per se but of its misuse for speculative purposes.

towner.source: Philip H. Towner, 1-2 Timothy, Titus, NICNT (2006) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The opening formula adapts Pauline convention to the Pastorals’ distinct social setting. Calling God 'Savior' situates the letter within the Greco-Roman world where soteriological language was politically charged. The epithet simultaneously critiques imperial soteriology and grounds Christian hope in divine rather than human deliverance. Timothy as 'true child in the faith' establishes a relationship of legitimate succession critical for the letter’s strategy of delegated authority.

The Ephesian situation involves teachers whose speculations have crossed from legitimate theological inquiry into territory that undermines community formation. The contrast between their εκζητήσεις (speculations/controversies) and God’s οἰκονομία (plan/stewardship) in v. 4 frames the choice starkly: speculative myth-making vs. the divine economy of salvation received by faith. The triple criterion of love, good conscience, and sincere faith functions as a pastoral diagnostic for evaluating any teaching.

hist.context: Ephesus in the mid-60s AD was a cosmopolitan center where Jewish and Hellenistic religious traditions intersected. The false teaching Paul combats appears to combine Jewish elements (myths, genealogies, law-speculation in vv. 4, 7) with an ascetic tendency that will become explicit in 4:1–5. The reference to genealogies (γενεαλογίαι) may point to speculative expansions of OT family trees (possibly proto-Gnostic emanation schemes) or Hellenistic Jewish haggadic traditions. Ephesus was already familiar with religious syncretism, as Paul’s earlier ministry there involved confrontation with both Jewish exorcists and devotees of Artemis (Acts 19).

sections.header: Verses 8–11 — "The Law Is Good If One Uses It Lawfully"

heb.paragraph: An adverb derived from νόμος (nomos, law), creating a deliberate wordplay: the law is good if used ‘law-fully’—that is, in accordance with its intended purpose. BDAG defines it as ‘in conformity with rules or standards.’ Paul’s point is not antinomian; the law retains a valid function in exposing sin, but it was never designed as a vehicle for speculative genealogies or self-righteous display.

refs.note: Paul’s earlier reflection on the law’s purpose—not to produce righteousness but to reveal sin—provides the theological foundation for his insistence here that the law is good when used properly.
The law as a guardian (παιδαγωγός) leading to Christ parallels the Pastorals’ view that the law’s function is pedagogical and revelatory, not soteriological.

notes.note: The law is not the enemy—it is the misuse of the law that is the problem. The false teachers treated the law as a springboard for speculation rather than a mirror that reveals sin.

Paul’s vice list reads like a commentary on the Ten Commandments. The law was given to restrain and convict sinners, not to produce self-righteous teachers who parade their knowledge while missing the law’s moral core.
All genuine teaching must conform to the glorious gospel. Any doctrine that contradicts or supplements the gospel with human speculation is by definition unsound.

Paul concedes that the law is good but insists it must be used rightly. The law was given to convict and restrain sinners, not to feed vain curiosity. When teachers abuse the law by turning it into a playground for endless speculation, they pervert its God-given purpose. The ultimate standard is the gospel of the blessed God, to which all teaching must conform.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'use it properly' (νομίμως χρῆται) contains a wordplay on νόμος: one must use the law ‘lawfully,’ i.e., in accordance with its intended function of exposing sin rather than fueling speculation.
The term 'sound doctrine' (ὑγιαινούσῃ διδασκαλίᾳ) uses a medical metaphor: doctrine that is ‘healthy’ or ‘wholesome,’ in contrast to the ‘sick’ obsessions of the false teachers (cf. 6:4).

mounce.source: William D. Mounce, Pastoral Epistles, WBC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The concessive clause (‘we know the law is good’) prevents any antinomian reading. Paul’s point is functional: the law was designed for the ἄνομος (lawless) and ἀνυπότακτος (rebellious), not for the righteous person who has internalized its moral demand through the Spirit.

The vice list loosely follows the Decalogue structure: lawless/rebellious (commandments 1–4), murderers of fathers and mothers (5th), sexually immoral/homosexuals (7th), slave traders (8th), liars/perjurers (9th). This Decalogue-shaped list demonstrates that Paul’s view of the law’s proper use is deeply rooted in its covenantal structure, not abstract moralism.

towner.source: Philip H. Towner, 1-2 Timothy, Titus, NICNT (2006) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The vice catalogue functions simultaneously as moral taxonomy and social rhetoric. By linking the opponents’ teaching to behaviors universally condemned in Greco-Roman moral philosophy, Paul delegitimizes their position not only theologically but socially. The terminal phrase ‘according to the gospel of the glory of the blessed God’ establishes the gospel—not the law, and certainly not speculative tradition—as the ultimate criterion of sound teaching.

hist.context: Paul’s nuanced view of the Torah’s function here echoes Romans 7:12 (‘the law is holy, righteous and good’) while insisting on its proper use. The vice list in vv. 9–10 follows a pattern loosely aligned with the Decalogue, suggesting Paul sees the law’s primary function as identifying and condemning sin. The phrase ‘the glorious gospel of the blessed God’ (v. 11) is striking in its doxological language and sets the gospel as the standard against which all teaching must be measured.

sections.header: Verses 12–20 — "Christ Jesus Came into the World to Save Sinners"

heb.paragraph: This formula (πιστός ὁ λόγος) appears five times in the Pastoral Epistles (1 Tim 1:15; 3:1; 4:9; 2 Tim 2:11; Tit 3:8) and nowhere else in the NT. It introduces creedal or hymnic material that the community already knows and affirms. Here it marks the soteriological core of the letter: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. Whether these are pre-Pauline formulas, Pauline compositions, or post-Pauline traditions is debated, but their liturgical character is clear.

Paul calls himself the πρῶτος (foremost, chief) of sinners—not merely the worst chronologically but the paradigmatic sinner. BDAG gives the sense as ‘most prominent.’ Paul’s self-designation functions as a theological argument: if the worst sinner can receive mercy, then no one is beyond the reach of Christ’s saving work.

refs.note: Paul’s conversion on the Damascus road is the historical event underlying his self-description as the foremost of sinners who received mercy—a paradigm of sovereign grace.

Paul’s earlier confession of being the least of the apostles because he persecuted the church provides a parallel to his ‘chief of sinners’ language here, though the Pastorals intensify the self-abasement.
Hymenaeus reappears in Paul’s second letter to Timothy, still teaching falsehood (that the resurrection has already occurred), confirming the persistence of the Ephesian heresy.

notes.note: Paul gives glory to Christ for his conversion and ministry. He was a blasphemer, a persecutor, and a violent man—yet he received mercy because he acted in ignorance and unbelief. Grace was not merely sufficient; it was superabundant.

Here is the first of five trustworthy sayings in the Pastorals. It is the clearest, simplest summary of the gospel in all of Scripture: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. Every other doctrine must orbit around this central truth.

Paul’s conversion is a display case of God’s patience—a prototype for all who would believe. The doxology that follows (v. 17) is the only proper response to such grace: to the King eternal, immortal, invisible, the only God, be honor and glory for ever and ever.

Paul names names. Hymenaeus and Alexander have shipwrecked their faith and been handed over to Satan for discipline. This is not vindictive but remedial—the goal is that they may learn not to blaspheme. Church discipline is an act of love, not cruelty.

Paul recounts his former life not to boast but to magnify the grace that overcame his rebellion. He was the chief of sinners, yet God displayed unlimited patience in him as a pattern for future believers. If the worst persecutor of the church can be saved, no sinner need despair of God’s mercy.

The charge to fight the good fight implies that the Christian life is warfare. Hymenaeus and Alexander serve as cautionary examples: they rejected good conscience and made shipwreck of their faith. Paul’s handing them to Satan is a form of excommunication—severe but intended for their ultimate restoration.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The 'trustworthy saying' formula (πιστός ὁ λόγος) is unique to the Pastoral Epistles and likely introduces pre-formed creedal or catechetical material known to the community.
The phrase 'handed over to Satan' parallels 1 Cor 5:5 and refers to exclusion from the congregation, which is viewed as the sphere of God’s protection; outside it one is exposed to Satan’s domain.

mounce.source: William D. Mounce, Pastoral Epistles, WBC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Paul’s thanksgiving (vv. 12–17) interrupts the charge but is theologically integral: it grounds the gospel he commissions Timothy to defend in personal experience. The triad βλάσφημος/διώκτης/ὑβριστής (blasphemer/persecutor/violent man) echoes Gal 1:13 and Phil 3:6, confirming authentic Pauline self-reflection.

The first πιστός λόγος introduces what may be a pre-Pauline confession. Paul’s self-identification as πρῶτος of sinners is not rhetorical hyperbole but theological conviction: his pre-conversion violence against the church makes him the paradigmatic beneficiary of divine μακροθυμία (patience). The doxology (v. 17) uses language characteristic of Hellenistic Jewish worship.

The military metaphor (στρατεύῃ την καλην στρατείαν, ‘wage the good warfare’) frames Timothy’s ministry as combat against heresy. The ‘handing over to Satan’ (παρέδωκα τῷ Σατανᾷ) parallels 1 Cor 5:5 and denotes excommunication with restorative intent.

towner.source: Philip H. Towner, 1-2 Timothy, Titus, NICNT (2006) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The autobiographical excursus serves a dual function: it validates the gospel Timothy must defend (by demonstrating its transformative power) and provides a paradigm of divine mercy that undercuts the opponents’ exclusive, knowledge-based soteriology. Paul’s emphasis on his ignorance (ἀγνοῶν) as a ground for mercy is theologically significant: it implies that the false teachers, who sin with knowledge, face greater culpability.

The naming of Hymenaeus and Alexander functions as a public warning to the Ephesian community. The language of ‘shipwreck’ (εναυάγησαν) draws on maritime imagery common in Greco-Roman moral discourse to describe catastrophic moral failure. The disciplinary action of ‘handing over to Satan’ reflects a two-sphere cosmology in which expulsion from the community entails exposure to hostile spiritual forces—a practice attested also in 1 Cor 5:5 and the Qumran community (1QS 2:4–18).

hist.context: Paul’s autobiographical digression (vv. 12–17) is not mere personal reminiscence but a carefully constructed paradigm. By narrating his own transformation from blasphemer and persecutor (cf. Acts 8:3; 9:1–2; Gal 1:13) to apostle, he demonstrates the very gospel he commissions Timothy to defend. The closing charge (vv. 18–20) names Hymenaeus and Alexander as examples of those who have ‘shipwrecked’ their faith—the only individuals named in connection with the Ephesian heresy. Hymenaeus reappears in 2 Tim 2:17, suggesting ongoing influence; Alexander may be the coppersmith of 2 Tim 4:14.

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Salutation: apostolic authority and filial relationship

rows.label: vv.3–7
rows.text: Charge against false teachers in Ephesus

rows.label: vv.8–11
rows.text: The proper use of the law vs. the gospel

lit.note: Chapter 1 moves from apostolic greeting (vv. 1–2) through the presenting problem (vv. 3–7) to a theological digression on law and gospel (vv. 8–11), a personal testimony of grace (vv. 12–17), and back to the original charge with named offenders (vv. 18–20). This ABA′ structure frames the gospel as both the content Timothy must teach and the power that transforms sinners.

themes.note: Mercy dominates through Paul’s paradigmatic conversion story (vv. 12–17), while Faith and Mission run throughout the charge to Timothy to defend sound doctrine. Judgment surfaces in the vice list and the discipline of Hymenaeus and Alexander.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Timothy
ppl.role: Paul’s young protégé; co-author of six NT letters
ppl.text: Timothy is recruited at Lystra on the second journey (Acts 16:1–3) — the son of a Jewish mother and Greek father, circumcised by Paul to aid Jewish outreach. He becomes Paul’s most trusted emissary, sent to troubled churches when Paul cannot go. Paul twice calls him "my true son in the faith" and addresses two personal letters to him. He is co-author of six Pauline letters and appears at the end of Hebrews.

ppl.name: Hymenaeus
ppl.text: 18–20) names Hymenaeus and Alexander as examples of those who have ‘shipwrecked’ their faith—the only individuals named in connection with the Ephesian heresy…

ppl.name: Alexander
ppl.text: 18–20) names Hymenaeus and Alexander as examples of those who have ‘shipwrecked’ their faith—the only individuals named in connection with the Ephesian heresy…

ppl.name: Christ Jesus
ppl.text: Paul’s apostleship rests on the direct command of God our Savior and Christ Jesus our hope…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Charge Against False Teaching

translations.text: They want to be teachers of the law, but they do not know what they are talking about or what they so confidently affirm.
desiring to be teachers of the law, without understanding either what they are saying or the things about which they make confident assertions.
We also know that the law is made not for the righteous but for lawbreakers and rebels, the ungodly and sinful, the unholy and irreligious, for those who kill their fathers or mothers, for murderers,

understanding this, that the law is not laid down for the just but for the lawless and disobedient, for the ungodly and sinners, for the unholy and profane, for those who strike their fathers and mothers, for murderers,

rec.quote: "Paul’s apostleship rests on the direct command of God our Savior and Christ Jesus our hope. This is no self-appointed ministry; it is a divine commission that cannot be revoked by human opposition or …"

rec.title: John Calvin

thread.text: Paul’s insistence on a single authoritative gospel and his anathema against those who preach a different one provides the theological precedent for his command against ἑτεροδιδασκαλία here.
The parallel warning against Jewish myths in the letter to Titus confirms that the same type of false teaching was spreading across multiple Pauline churches during this period.
Paul’s farewell speech to the Ephesian elders predicted that savage wolves would arise from within the church—a prophecy now being fulfilled in the very community Timothy oversees.
Paul’s earlier reflection on the law’s purpose—not to produce righteousness but to reveal sin—provides the theological foundation for his insistence here that the law is good when used properly.
The law as a guardian (παιδαγωγός) leading to Christ parallels the Pastorals’ view that the law’s function is pedagogical and revelatory, not soteriological.
Paul’s conversion on the Damascus road is the historical event underlying his self-description as the foremost of sinners who received mercy—a paradigm of sovereign grace.

coaching.tip: Paul warns against teachers who promote 'myths and endless genealogies' that produce 'speculations rather than stewardship' (v. 4). Doctrine matters not for intellectual satisfaction but for godly living ('love that issues from a pure heart'). The test of teaching isn't sophistication but transformation. Does it make people more like Christ?

'Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am the foremost' (v. 15). Paul doesn't say 'I was' but 'I am' — present tense. The apostle who wrote half the New Testament still considers himself chief sinner. This isn't false modesty but clear sight: the closer to God, the more aware of remaining sin. Growth increases humility.

Paul received mercy 'so that in me, as the foremost, Jesus Christ might display his perfect patience as an example' (v. 16). His conversion is an exhibit — if God saved Saul the persecutor, no one is beyond reach. Paul's story isn't just his; it's apologetic evidence. Personal testimony becomes theological argument.
